slides.logical_links: This is the cover slide of the deck. The large title 'Malbon China Y26-Y27 Proposal' names the client (Malbon), the market (China) and the timeframe (Y26-Y27), establishing the document as a forward-looking strategy proposal. The subtitle 'by WalktheChat' sits directly beneath it, attributing authorship to the agency. The two stacked, left-aligned text blocks read top to bottom with no competing imagery, so all attention is on the engagement framing.

slides.title: Strategy Overview

children.text: From Global Brand Story to China Growth Strategy

slides.logical_links: This is a section divider opening the strategy portion of the deck. The heading 'Strategy Overview' labels the upcoming section, and the bold 28pt subtitle 'From Global Brand Story to China Growth Strategy' frames the narrative arc the section will follow: translating Malbon's existing global identity into a concrete China growth plan. slides.title: Golf participation is growing, and indoor venues are making access more repeatable.

text_hierarchy.text: Golf participation is growing, and indoor venues are making access more repeatable.

children.text: Comparable on-course data shows steady growth, while indoor rounds are scaling much faster — expanding golf from occasional course participation into more urban and accessible play occasions.
Traditional golf participation continues to grow steadily.
The core golf base remains meaningful — but growth is steady rather than explosive.
Urban venues are turning golf into a more frequent and lower-threshold experience.
Indoor venues are becoming a practical entry engine for new players, social occasions and repeat participation.

text_hierarchy.text: Golf access is moving from “exclusive course participation” toward a broader urban participation funnel.
Strategic interpretation: golf access is shifting from exclusive course occasions toward a broader urban participation funnel.
Sources: Ping An Securities / Huajing Industry Research Institute; Guanyan Tianxia; China Golf Association / China Sports Daily.

images.description: A young woman in a white visor, cream top and beige trousers captured mid golf swing on a lush green outdoor course, illustrating traditional on-course play.
A young woman in a navy top and white pleated skirt swinging a club in front of an indoor golf simulator screen showing a projected fairway, illustrating indoor venue play.

slides.logical_links: The slide is a two-column comparative data story whose bold headline asserts that golf participation is growing while indoor venues make access more repeatable. The left column ('On-course participation keeps rising') pairs the outdoor swing photo with a bar series rising 93.7→98.5 from 2018–2023 and a +5.1% callout, supporting its callout that the core base is meaningful but grows steadily. The right column ('Indoor golf is scaling faster') pairs the indoor-simulator photo with rounds climbing 4.61→10.07 from 2021–2024 and a much larger +118% figure, supporting its callout that indoor venues are an entry engine. The deliberate contrast between the modest +5.1% and dramatic +118% is the slide's core argument, resolved in the bottom rounded-rectangle synthesis that golf access is shifting from exclusive course participation toward a broader urban participation funnel, with sources footnoted at the very bottom.

slides.title: Golfcore is making golf discoverable before consumers become players.

text_hierarchy.text: Golfcore is making golf discoverable before consumers become players.

children.text: China Golf Market Observation: Social Interest & Youth Signal
For many young consumers, the first entry into golf may be style, content and social identity — before full participation in the sport.
views on Xiaohongshu golf outfit-related terms
Golf style is already breaking beyond core players and entering platform culture.

Xiaohongshu Search Growth
Y24 vs Y25

Douyin Search Growth
Y24 vs Y25
Users aged 23–35 account for over 50% of the golf-interest audience

text_hierarchy.text: Malbon can convert golfcore attention into cultural entry — from wearing the code to joining the game.
Sources: Xinhua / Beijing Business Today; Xiaohongshu Ball Sports Trend Forecast; Future Market Insights.

images.description: A Xiaohongshu app screenshot of the hashtag feed '#高尔夫穿搭' (golf outfit) with 5.1亿 views, showing a grid of posts featuring women in golf attire, golf bags and colorful accessories.
A Xiaohongshu app screenshot of the hashtag feed '#golfcore' with 265.3万 views, showing a grid of stylish golf-outfit lifestyle posts of young women on and around courses.

slides.logical_links: The slide argues that 'golfcore' (golf as fashion/content) is a discovery funnel that precedes actual play, set up by the headline and the body line that style, content and social identity are the first entry point for young consumers. The two centered Xiaohongshu screenshots (#高尔夫穿搭 and #golfcore) provide platform-native proof of this content culture and anchor the left '500M views' KPI block, which states golf style is breaking beyond core players. The right-hand stack of three KPIs escalates the evidence — +32% Xiaohongshu and +45.9% Douyin search growth (Y24 vs Y25) show rising demand, while >50% of the golf-interest audience being aged 23–35 confirms the youth signal. The bottom rounded-rectangle callout converts all this into the strategic implication: Malbon can turn golfcore attention into cultural entry, 'from wearing the code to joining the game,' with sources and page number 04 at the foot.

slides.title: Although golf has gained more visibility, it still feels difficult to enter and lacks appeal for new users.

text_hierarchy.text: Although golf has gained more visibility, it still feels difficult to enter and lacks appeal for new users.

children.text: The market shift creates a contradiction: young consumers are attracted by golf’s lifestyle appeal but still feel unsure if they belong in golf culture.
72% of users believe that the high cost of golf equipment and club memberships is not only a basic requirement to get into the sport, but also a way people show social status and identity*
“I really wish there were more good-looking pants. The ones in stores are either too short or just not nice…, and the skirts aren’t great either.”
“Everything is just white and blue now, maybe a slightly brighter yellow at best. The colors and patterns are all so boring. I can’t even find anything in neon colors that I want to buy.”
Unlike F1 or tennis, it doesn’t have strong cultural icons that break into popularity

text_hierarchy.text: Source: CBNData’s 2025 Sports & Outdoor Interest Group Consumer Insights Paper, N=1,500

images.description: A young man in a black golf polo and white trousers holding a glove, standing on a green course, conveying golf's premium, status-oriented image.
A cropped Chinese social-media comment screenshot complaining that golf pants in stores are short and ugly and that Korean trend brands' skirts are too pricey.
A cropped Chinese social-media comment screenshot complaining that golf apparel is now all white and blue with boring colors and patterns and no neon options.
An 'F1' movie poster featuring Brad Pitt in white racing gear standing beside a crashed race car, used as an example of a sport with a strong cultural icon.
A red-toned 'Champion' tennis poster of a female player mid-action with an Olympic rings logo, used as another example of a sport with breakout cultural icons.

slides.logical_links: This slide diagnoses the cultural tension that counterbalances the prior opportunity slides: despite golf's rising visibility, it remains hard to enter, framed by the headline and the body contradiction that young consumers want the lifestyle but feel unsure they belong. The body splits into three side-by-side barrier columns. 'Status Barrier' is supported by the premium male-golfer photo and the 72% statistic that cost signals social status. 'Lack of style and variety' is evidenced by two real Chinese consumer-comment screenshots griping about ugly, repetitive white-and-blue apparel. 'No Ice Breaker' is illustrated by the F1 (Brad Pitt) and tennis 'Champion' posters as contrasting sports that do have breakout cultural icons, reinforcing that golf lacks one. The three barriers read left to right as the obstacles Malbon must overcome, with the CBNData source asterisked at the foot supporting the 72% figure.

slides.title: Malbon opens up golf culture.

children.text: Malbon was not built to simply make golf apparel. It was built to make golf feel fresh, welcoming and culturally alive for today’s generation.
It respects the game but does not let old codes decide who belongs. It gives players and newcomers permission to participate in golf culture in their own way.
Relevant on and off the course; from fairway to city life.
Not followers, but fellow players connected by a shared culture.

images.description: An aerial shot of a sweeping green fairway with golfers and bags walking up the hole, annotated with playful labels like '17-Year MLB Vetran', 'Multi-Platinum Recording Artist', 'Augusta National Invitee', 'First Time Golfing' and '456-Yard Driver', conveying a mixed, inclusive community of players.
A professional golfer in a white quarter-zip and light-blue trousers walking the fairway holding a putter during a tournament, representing golf credibility and roots in the game.

An editorial lifestyle shot of two models on stone steps — a woman in a black jumpsuit in front and a man in a green 'Malbon' varsity jacket and white shorts behind — conveying golf-rooted style worn off the course in city life.

slides.logical_links: This slide defines Malbon's brand essence as 'Malbon opens up golf culture,' elaborated by the two body lines stating the brand was built to make golf feel fresh and welcoming and to give newcomers permission to belong. The essence is then operationalized into three pillars read left to right that map directly onto the three images above them: 'Golf Root' (Built from the game) sits over the tournament golfer photo conveying credibility; 'Lifestyle Expression' (from fairway to city life) sits over the editorial off-course style shot in Malbon apparel; and 'Community Belonging' (fellow players connected by shared culture) sits over the annotated fairway GIF showing a mixed cast from a first-time golfer to a recording artist. The image-to-pillar pairing visually proves each abstract value, building the bridge from credibility through style to community that the rest of the strategy will leverage.

slides.title: Malbon can make golf easier to enter without making it less aspirational.

text_hierarchy.text: Malbon can make golf easier to enter without making it less aspirational.

children.text: The opportunity is not to become a more professional golf brand, or a more golf-looking lifestyle brand — but to own the space between golf credibility and lifestyle entry.
Professional and credible, but often formal, technical and distant.
The cultural entry point into golf lifestyle in China.
Stylish and culturally relevant, but often only borrowing golf aesthetics.

slides.logical_links: This positioning slide frames Malbon's strategic white space, with the headline asserting it can make golf easier to enter without losing aspiration and the body defining the goal as owning the space between golf credibility and lifestyle entry. The layout is a three-column spectrum: on the left, 'Traditional golf brands' are professional and credible but formal, technical and distant (existing-golfer focused); on the right, 'Lifestyle / streetwear brands' are stylish and relevant but only borrow golf aesthetics (style-led, not golf-rooted). The center column, 'Malbon’s space,' resolves the two extremes with the triad 'Rooted in the game, expressed through style, and sustained by community,' and its strategic role as 'the cultural entry point into golf lifestyle in China.' The reading flow moves from the two flanking poles inward to Malbon's reconciling middle position, with page number 06 at the foot.

slides.title: 「城市果岭 风格无界」Turns golf-rooted identity into a lifestyle community.

text_hierarchy.text: 「城市果岭 风格无界」Turns golf-rooted identity into a lifestyle community.

children.text: Malbon’s credibility starts from the game — its rituals, mindset, self-challenge and connection.
The city extends the green beyond the course, making golf part of stores, coffee, travel, indoor golf and social moments.
Product design, golfcore appeal and collaborations with art, music and fashion invite more people into Malbon’s world.
Malbon gathers people who like the product, believe in the attitude and are curious to participate — turning shared taste into brand belonging.

text_hierarchy.text: Malbon starts from the green, expands into city lifestyle, invites people through style and cultural expression, then gathers them into a community asset.

slides.logical_links: The slide presents Malbon's 2026 annual theme '城市果岭 风格无界' (Urban Green, Boundless Style) as a left-to-right progression model. The title and its subtitle frame the strategic objective of turning a golf-rooted identity into a lifestyle community. Below, four sequential cards read as a flow: 'Golf Root' (credibility) 'expands' into 'City Lifestyle Expansion' and 'Style & Cultural Expression', which together 'build into' the final 'Community Asset' card — the connector labels 'expands' and 'builds into' make the cause-effect chain explicit. The 'What people identify with first' heading groups the first three cards as entry points, while the larger Community Asset card is the endpoint outcome. The bottom callout bar restates the whole sequence in one sentence as the slide's takeaway.

slides.title: Social, campaign and offline should work together to move people from curiosity to participation, then from participation to belonging.

text_hierarchy.text: Social, campaign and offline should work together to move people from curiosity to participation, then from participation to belonging.

children.text: Build a diverse content system around golf spirit, lifestyle moments, styling-led expression and community participation — making Malbon easier to understand and closer to daily life.
Use hero campaigns, KOL stories and paid amplification to turn “Urban Green, Boundless Style” into high-impact narratives that reach beyond core golfers and build lifestyle aspiration.
Transform stores, ranges, indoor golf, pop-ups and city gatherings into accessible entry points where people experience golf culture, meet fellow players and return as part of the community.

slides.logical_links: This slide operationalizes the previous theme into a three-part 'Toolkit'. The title states the strategic objective: three levers working together to advance people along a journey of Curiosity → Participation → Belonging, which appear as stage labels across the top. Each stage sits above one of three parallel columns — Social Content (under Curiosity), Campaign & Collaboration (under Participation), and Offline Experience (under Belonging) — establishing a one-to-one mapping between the customer-journey stage and the channel that drives it. Each column carries a bold imperative tagline ('Make the culture visible / memorable / real') followed by an explanatory body paragraph, so the columns are read as parallel tactics that escalate in commitment from passive viewing to physical, real-world participation.

slides.title: We are not targeting “golfers” only — we are building Malbon’s China world.

text_hierarchy.text: We are not targeting “golfers” only — we are building Malbon’s China world.

children.text: Real golfers who validate Malbon’s golf credibility.
Golfcore / lifestyle consumers entering through dressing and social content.
Artists, photographers, buyers, coffee people, musicians and creators.
Coaches, local KOCs, range hosts and golf community organizers.
Women attracted by style, experience and beginner-friendly entry points.

text_hierarchy.text: Each audience plays a different role in making Malbon understood, desired and joined.

slides.logical_links: The slide argues that Malbon's audience extends well beyond literal golfers, as stated in the title, and breaks the target into five numbered segments laid out left to right. Each segment pairs a number (01–05), a name, and a one-line role description, defining a distinct persona: Core Players (credibility), Style Players (fashion entry), Cultural Connectors (creative reach), Community Builders (grassroots organizers), and Female Golf-Curious (a growth segment). The numbering implies a deliberate ordering from the credibility core outward to aspirational and growth audiences. The bottom callout ties the five together by explaining their collective function — making Malbon 'understood, desired and joined' — which echoes the curiosity-to-belonging logic of the prior slides.

slides.title: Malbon’s brand accounts are visually strong but need a clearer content structure to drive discovery and engagement.

text_hierarchy.text: Malbon’s brand accounts are visually strong but need a clearer content structure to drive discovery and engagement.

children.text: WeChat has richer SVG interactions and editorial layouts; RED benefits from premium product and styling assets that naturally suit visual-first browsing.
Maintain a consistent posting frequency to keep the official account active and relevant.
Optimize the WeChat menu structure to improve access to brand updates, product drops, store information and community content.
Leverage brand campaign/moment to activate engagement campaigns to build a stronger brand hub.
Expand content formats beyond product and styling visuals to build a richer brand narrative.
Develop clear content columns with consistent naming, so new users can quickly understand and browse Malbon’s key content areas.

images.description: A mobile screenshot of a Malbon Golf WeChat/RED post titled '上海, Game On!' from Malbon Golf, dated 2026年1月24日, set in Shanghai. The dark maroon editorial graphic shows a line-drawn cityscape with an LA KC building and a large stylized 'm' monogram forming a winding golf-course path, with Chinese copy about a white 'signal ball' arcing across the Pacific and landing in central Shanghai ('城市,就是我们的新果岭 / 这一次,Buckets 为你而来'), plus a 'Final Approach' label; engagement icons (9 likes, 18 reposts, 7, 2) appear at the bottom.

A mobile screenshot of a Malbon RED (Xiaohongshu) '笔记' feed grid showing four note thumbnails in a two-column layout: a collage with golf and fashion imagery captioned about 'pension aesthetics', a woman in a cap and dark polo sipping coffee on a course (caption referencing Gallo Colorido), a 'Malbon Home message from Augusta' post with 134 likes, and a vintage golf crowd photo; all posted under the 'Malbon' account with like counts (76, 134, etc.).

slides.logical_links: The slide assesses Malbon's existing China social accounts, with the title delivering the core judgment: visually strong but lacking content structure. The two mobile screenshots on the left provide visual evidence — a polished WeChat editorial post ('上海, Game On!') and a RED note-feed grid — demonstrating the 'visually strong' premise. The right-hand text block then splits the analysis into 'What is working' (richer SVG/editorial on WeChat, premium visual assets on RED) and 'What needs to improve', the latter broken per platform: WeChat needs consistent posting, a better menu, and campaign-driven engagement, while RED needs broader content formats and clearly named content columns. The images thus anchor the assessment, and the body translates the visual-strength-but-weak-structure thesis into concrete per-platform fixes.

slides.title: Channel suggestion: lean more resources on RED for brand building and performance-driven actions.

text_hierarchy.text: Channel suggestion: lean more resources on RED for brand building and performance-driven actions.

children.text:
Product and brand discovery and review.
Used as a dynamic info search engine.

“She-economy”: 70% active female users.
No.1 platform for creator marketing.
High purchasing power.
Good for celebrity collaboration, but engagement is declining.

Good for mass targeting to grow awareness.
Requires frequent livestreaming to perform.

text_hierarchy.text: RED should be the core platform to build discoverability, creator credibility and purchase intent.

slides.logical_links: This is a comparison matrix that backs the title's recommendation to concentrate resources on RED. The table's left column lists five evaluation dimensions (DIMENSION, ROLE, STRENGTH, OUR SUGGESTION, POSTS / MONTH) and the four platform columns (WeChat, RED, Weibo, Douyin) are each tagged with a trend arrow — RED and Douyin '↑', Weibo '↓', WeChat '→' — signaling momentum. Reading down each column lets the reader compare role, strength, recommended action, and cadence side by side: RED is singled out as 'FOCUS / Active' with 12 posts/month thanks to its she-economy and creator-marketing strengths, WeChat is to 'Maintain' as a brand hub (4 posts), while Weibo and Douyin are deferred to a 'Later stage'. The bottom callout bar restates the conclusion that RED should be the core platform, making the matrix the evidence for the channel prioritization.

text_hierarchy.text: The content system should make Malbon more searchable, more understandable and more participatory across RED and WeChat.

images.description: A cinematic movie-style poster titled 'COMMON GROUNDS' in large white type, framed as 'A Malbon Golf film', showing a moody dark cafe/clubhouse interior with a coffee setup (silver pot, cups, condiments) on a counter; credit-block text and a Malbon logo appear at the bottom.

A product/editorial shot of a woman wearing a navy Malbon short-sleeve knit polo top and matching pleated skirt with white piping, photographed among tropical jungle foliage with other figures around her — a styled lookbook image for a clothing drop.

A lifestyle photo of a woman on a sunlit golf course wearing a navy 'LEISURE' hoodie, white pleated skirt, cap and white sneakers, holding a club with a golf push-cart beside her; small Chinese caption text ('轻松上场·自在享受其中') overlays the image.
A product shot of a pair of green-and-white Malbon suede sneakers floating against a clear blue sky, styled as a clean collaboration/fashion crossover image.

A LIV Golf-branded sports poster of golfer Anthony Kim mid-stride on a course, with bold headline text 'FOR ANYONE WHO’S STRUGGLING, YOU CAN GET THROUGH ANYTHING' and his name/credentials, plus his handwritten signature at the bottom.

A green 'Malbon' collage graphic showing a stylized China map with city pins (广州, 北京, 上海, 深圳, 武汉, 杭州) and several inset photos of people in Malbon outfits posing in different city locations, captioned '期待解锁更多城市打卡' (looking forward to unlocking more city check-ins).

slides.logical_links: The slide lays out Malbon's six proposed content pillars as a horizontal row, each presented as a paired image (top) and text card (bottom) in matching columns. From left to right the pillars are 果岭笔记 / Golf Culture & Brand POV (illustrated by the 'COMMON GROUNDS' brand film poster), 新品入局 / Product & Drop (the navy outfit product-shot), 城市果岭 / Lifestyle Scenario (the on-course leisure lifestyle photo), 风格无界 / Culture & Collaboration (the green collab sneakers), Bucket Club / Community & Sports Marketing (the LIV Golf Anthony Kim athlete poster), and 球友召集 / Engagement Campaign (the Malbon city-check-in map collage). Each image is a concrete visual example of the territory its card defines below it, and each card lists representative content types under a bilingual Chinese name and English descriptor. The bottom callout bar states the overall purpose — making Malbon more searchable, understandable and participatory across RED and WeChat — framing the six pillars as a single coherent always-on content system per the title.

slides.title: Brand-oriented content can make the brand easier to understand through lighter, more human storytelling.

text_hierarchy.text: Brand-oriented content can make the brand easier to understand through lighter, more human storytelling.
The 6 posts under @HONMA’s brand story collection generated 120K+ total engagements, with 5 out of 6 posts receiving over 1K likes and becoming viral posts.
@MARIMEKKO used an office vlog format to show the brand’s HQ culture, turning internal daily moments into approachable brand storytelling and helping audiences feel closer to the brand.

images.description: A vertical RED (Xiaohongshu) screenshot of @HONMA's '品牌故事' (Brand Story) video collection list. It shows a series of short-video thumbnails with Chinese titles, durations (e.g. 02:32, 02:54, 00:43) and view/like/comment counts, plus a 'collect this set' button at the bottom — a curated brand-story playlist.

A RED post detail view from MARIMEKKO's account. Left side shows a food/breakfast vlog frame with the caption overlay 'POV: You work at Marimekko HQ' (工作Vlog | Marimekko總部午餐日常); right side shows the Chinese caption text and a comment thread, illustrating an office-vlog brand-storytelling format.

A vertical RED video thumbnail from MARIMEKKO showing a person in a printed jacket walking through the brand's HQ corridor with pink walls and an iconic Unikko-print sofa. Overlay text reads 'POV: you work at Marimekko HQ' and the Chinese title '在Marimekko总部上班是什么体验？' with 2116 likes.

slides.logical_links: The title argues that lighter, human storytelling makes a brand easier to understand, and the two case columns below prove it with real benchmarks. The left @HONMA screenshot (img1) and its caption show how a structured 'brand story' video collection drove 120K+ engagements with 5 of 6 posts going viral, demonstrating the payoff of narrative content. The right pair of MARIMEKKO screenshots (img2 detail post, img3 video thumbnail) together with the second caption illustrate a different tactic — an office-vlog format that humanizes the brand by showing HQ culture. Reading flows left-to-right from one proof case (HONMA storytelling) to another (Marimekko vlog), with each image visually anchored above the caption that explains its result.

slides.title: Moodboard-style content is a natural extension of Malbon’s origin, helping the brand continuously express its taste, attitude and values in a softer way.

text_hierarchy.text: Moodboard-style content is a natural extension of Malbon’s origin, helping the brand continuously express its taste, attitude and values in a softer way.
@AMI Paris curated playlist has received 3K+ listens, while the hashtag #AmiParis友你真好 generated around 500K views.
@Sporty & Rich uses playlists, moodboards and short statements to softly express its lifestyle values, attracting audiences who identify with the same taste, attitude and cultural references.

images.description: Side-by-side RED screenshots of an AMI Paris music campaign. Left is a QQ Music playlist card titled 'THANK YOU FOR BEING A FRIEND' / '友你真好' with a curated song list (Friends, I'll Be There For You); right is the matching RED post caption announcing the 'Ami Paris x QQ音乐' collaboration with a comment thread.

A Sporty & Rich playlist/moodboard card labeled 'No.3', featuring the Rodriguez album image 'Coming From Reality' with the track 'I Think Of You' and lyric line 'Down the streets I walked with you' — a curated music moodboard tile.
A Sporty & Rich moodboard image of Princess Diana in a pink cardigan with a white towel, overlaid with the statement 'Do not work more than you live' — a vintage-photo lifestyle/values tile.
A Sporty & Rich moodboard image of a woman in a white dress running on a beach, overlaid with the statement 'Don’t get attached' — another aspirational lifestyle/values tile.

slides.logical_links: The title frames moodboard-style content as a soft way to express taste, attitude and values, and the slide splits into two supporting columns. The left AMI Paris screenshot (img1) plus its caption show the 'playlist as moodboard' tactic, quantified by 3K+ listens and ~500K hashtag views. The right cluster of three Sporty & Rich tiles (img2 music card, img3 Diana statement, img4 beach statement) visually demonstrates the second caption's point that playlists, moodboards and short statements attract like-minded audiences. Reading order runs left (one brand case with metrics) to right (a second brand showing the full moodboard vocabulary), with the three stacked tiles collectively embodying the 'taste, attitude and cultural references' the caption describes.

slides.title: A strong product drop needs a full storyline from teaser to styling scenario to build product desire progressively.

text_hierarchy.text: A strong product drop needs a full storyline from teaser to styling scenario to build product desire progressively.
New Collection Launch: use PDPAOLA ICONS & ROUJE PALOMA as examples

children.text: Build pre-launch curiosity through product details, mood visuals and short campaign snippets.
Feature global campaign assets with platform-friendly captions and content sequencing, helping audiences better understand the collection.
Feature Malbon team narration on design inspiration, functional details, material choices and golf culture references.
Use KOL content to show the collection across different scenarios, from course to city life.

images.description: A small vertical ROUJE PALOMA campaign card showing a woman in a black-and-red floral skirt dancing flamenco-style in sunlight, with 'PALOMA' and 'COLLECTION' text — an editorial/teaser campaign visual.
A small vertical ROUJE PALOMA campaign card showing a woman in a tailored grey pinstripe suit with a red rose on a Parisian street, labeled 'PALOMA COLLECTION' — an editorial styling visual.
